name: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction
principles: time-varying magnetic flux induces electric field
Lenz's law (induced current opposes flux change)
Maxwell-Faraday integral / differential equivalence via Stokes' theorem
conventions: Phi_B = integral of B . dA over the open surface S; orientation of dA tied to direction of dl by right-hand rule
the line integral on the left is the EMF (electromotive force) induced around the loop, units of volts
minus sign encodes Lenz's law: induced EMF drives a current whose magnetic field opposes the original flux change
valid for any closed curve in vacuum or matter; in matter, replace E by macroscopic field and B by macroscopic B
stationary loop in time-varying B: pure transformer EMF; moving loop in static B: motional EMF — both unified by the same equation
expectedLimits: static fields (dB/dt = 0): induced EMF = 0; circulation of E vanishes; consistent with electrostatics
uniform B(t), planar loop of area A: EMF = -A dB/dt; standard transformer / inductor formula
loop moving with velocity v through static B: EMF = integral of (v x B) . dl; recovers motional EMF
self-inductance: EMF = -L dI/dt where L = Phi_B / I (geometry-dependent constant)
high-frequency limit: combined with Maxwell's displacement current term (Ampère-Maxwell), yields electromagnetic wave propagation
quasi-static approximation: valid when wavelength of EM disturbance >> circuit dimensions; otherwise full radiation treatment required
references: Faraday (1831) — Experimental Researches in Electricity, series I
Maxwell (1865) — A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field
